USD.catalyst: The U.S. dollar surged to a one-week high on July 13 following a sharp escalation in Middle East hostilities, including renewed U.S. strikes on Iran and the subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz. These geopolitical shocks have driven oil prices higher, fueling market expectations for further Federal Reserve tightening, with Fed funds futures now pricing a 52.1% probability of a rate hike. Investors are pivoting to the greenback as a primary safe haven while awaiting the upcoming CPI release to gauge the extent of energy-driven inflationary pressures. The currency remains supported by a widening 150bp carry advantage over the Euro and a significant data outperformance gap, as reflected in the USD CESI reaching +63.

sources.title: Dollar jumps on renewed Middle East attacks, Hormuz closure - CNBC
Dollar Jumps on Middle East Conflict, Hormuz Closure and Higher Oil - Global Banking & Finance Review
Dollar stands tall as Gulf tensions fuel oil price surge, Fed hike bets - CNBC

GBP.catalyst: Sterling is trading near 1.3379 as markets digest reports of an expansive autumn budget under Prime Minister Burnham, which may merge the fiscal statement with a spending review. While Pantheon Macroeconomics notes the UK economy is defying higher oil prices, the prospect of increased fiscal spending has introduced a layer of uncertainty that is reflected in a significant +5.5pt volatility risk premium in the options market. Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey recently addressed the market, asserting the bank's independence and ability to resist lobbying efforts following a private meeting with Nigel Farage regarding cryptocurrency policy. Despite these domestic headlines, the pound remains sensitive to broader Middle East tensions which have increased the risk of further BoE tightening to combat imported inflation.

sources.title: Pound Sterling: UK Economy Defies Higher Oil Prices, Says Pantheon - Exchange Rates UK
Bank of England governor says he is ‘able to spot’ lobbying after Farage crypto meeting - The Guardian

EUR.catalyst: The Euro weakened 0.1% against the dollar as persistent data misses and a widening 150bp carry disadvantage continue to weigh on the single currency. While French inflation was confirmed to have fallen in June, easing immediate pressure on the ECB, the currency remains vulnerable to energy-driven inflation risks stemming from the Gulf conflict. Speculative positioning remains bearish with COT leveraged funds holding a net short of 66,057 contracts, while the options market shows a +2.3pt volatility risk premium as traders hedge against further downside. The EUR/USD pair is currently trading near 1.1397, pressured by a negative CESI gap of -11 compared to the robust U.S. data profile.

sources.title: Pound Sterling: UK Economy Defies Higher Oil Prices, Says Pantheon - Exchange Rates UK

JPY.catalyst: The Japanese Yen remains under severe pressure, trading near 162.03 as the 275bp Fed-BoJ carry differential outweighs safe-haven bids typically associated with geopolitical crises. Despite the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the yen has failed to gain traction, with analysts like Steve Englander of Standard Chartered suggesting that coordinated U.S.-Japan FX intervention is unlikely. Market participants remain wary of Tokyo's intervention threshold as the currency languishes near 40-year lows, though Robin Brooks of the Brookings Institution warns that such moves may be doomed by Japan's debt-to-GDP ratio of 240%. Leveraged funds maintain a crowded net short position of 104,231 contracts, leaving the pair susceptible to a sharp squeeze only if the VIX breaches the 25.0 level.

sources.title: Dollar debasement arguments are misplaced and U.S.-Japan FX coordination won't happen: Analyst - CNBC

AUD.catalyst: The Australian Dollar is underperforming in the current session, softening below the 112.50 level against the yen as a cautious global market mood dampens demand for risk-sensitive currencies. The currency is struggling to find a firm footing as the escalation of conflict in the Middle East and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz drive a flight to the U.S. dollar. While the AUD has seen some idiosyncratic gains against other majors, it remains largely tethered to shifts in global commodity prices and regional risk sentiment. Without specific domestic data catalysts on July 13, price action is being dictated by the broader "risk-off" tone and the surge in safe-haven USD bids.

sources.title: Dollar jumps on renewed Middle East attacks, Hormuz closure - CNBC

CAD.catalyst: The Canadian Dollar reached a three-week high following a July 10 jobs report that showed the economy added 18,200 positions in June, nearly double the expected 10,000, while the unemployment rate fell to 6.5%. Despite a 4.19% spike in WTI oil prices due to the Hormuz closure, the traditional correlation between CAD and crude has collapsed to -0.899 as the market prioritizes USD safe-haven demand. Societe Generale strategists note that USD/CAD has met resistance near 1.4250, with key support now eyed at 1.4130 and the 50-day moving average at 1.3950. Leveraged funds remain heavily net short on the Loonie, though the recent employment beat has provided a fundamental buffer against broader USD strength.

sources.title: Canadian dollar heads for first weekly gain in six weeks after jobs beat - KITCO
Canadian Dollar: Firmer against US Dollar – Societe Generale - Bitget

CHF.catalyst: The Swiss Franc has exhibited relative weakness against the U.S. dollar as the latter captures the bulk of safe-haven flows following the resumption of U.S. attacks on Iran. Fed 3.75%. Domestic catalysts remain sparse, leaving the CHF to trade primarily on its status as a secondary haven and its sensitivity to Eurozone spillover risks. The currency remains range-bound against the Euro near 0.9500 as traders monitor the impact of rising energy costs on Swiss price stability.

NZD.catalyst: The New Zealand Dollar rose 0.5% to $0.5725, remaining well-bid following a hawkish shift from the Reserve Bank of New Zealand, which delivered its first interest rate hike in three years. This policy pivot has created a significant divergence with speculative positioning, as leveraged funds hold a net short of 27,675 contracts, setting the stage for a potential short squeeze toward the 0.5900 level. While the U.S. dollar's strength on the back of Iran strikes has capped gains, the RBNZ's new tightening bias provides the Kiwi with a unique fundamental tailwind among G10 peers. Traders are currently balancing the RBNZ's hawkish stance against the inflationary risks posed by the surge in global oil prices.

NOK.catalyst: The Norwegian Krone remains largely range-bound as the market weighs the massive spike in global crude prices against the broader flight to the U.S. dollar. While the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the resulting surge in oil prices are fundamentally supportive for the energy-exporting currency, the lack of fresh domestic policy signals from Norges Bank has limited idiosyncratic volatility. The Krone is currently tracking broader risk sentiment and energy market developments, pending new inflation data or central bank commentary to break current technical levels.

SEK.catalyst: The Swedish Krona is currently trading in a neutral range as it lacks specific domestic catalysts to decouple from broader European sentiment. The currency remains sensitive to the inflationary implications of the Middle East conflict and the resulting pressure on the ECB, which often dictates the Riksbank's policy trajectory. Without significant data releases or central bank speakers on the July 13 calendar, the Krona is expected to remain range-bound against the Euro and vulnerable to further depreciation against the U.S. dollar in the current "risk-off" environment.
